definition: The stage that distills human comparison judgments into a separate model predicting a scalar reward, so reinforcement learning can score thousands of fresh samples per step instead of querying humans. The whole alignment stage is bounded by its quality: a policy will find and exploit every flaw in the reward model, which is the mechanism behind reward hacking, and it is exactly the component direct preference methods remove.

body.text: Reward modeling turns a pile of human preference judgments into a differentiable score a policy can be optimized against. In the standard RLHF pipeline, humans cannot sit in the training loop rating every sample, so their judgments are first distilled into a model: the reward model takes a prompt and a candidate response and returns a single number meant to track how much a human would approve. Once it exists, the expensive, slow human is replaced by a cheap, fast function call, and reinforcement learning can score thousands of fresh on-policy samples per step.

The training signal is comparison, not absolute rating, because humans are far more reliable at saying which of two responses is better than at assigning a calibrated score to one in isolation. Labelers see a prompt with two or more completions and rank them, and the reward model is trained so the preferred response receives a higher scalar than the rejected one, using a pairwise loss derived from the Bradley-Terry model of comparisons, mathematically a logistic, cross-entropy style objective on the difference between the two predicted rewards. The model itself is usually the pretrained language model with its output layer replaced by a single regression head, so it inherits a strong representation of language before it ever sees a preference.

Reward modeling matters because the quality of the entire alignment stage is bounded by it. A policy optimized with reinforcement learning will find and exploit every flaw in the reward model, so a reward model that rewards length, sycophancy, or confident formatting will produce a policy that is verbose, fawning, or overconfident. This is the mechanism behind reward hacking: the policy maximizes the proxy, not the human intent the proxy was meant to capture, and as the policy drifts away from the data the reward model was trained on, the reward model's judgments grow less trustworthy, a failure called reward over-optimization, which is why RLHF keeps the policy anchored near a reference model and why fresh preference data is collected in iterative rounds.

The reward model is exactly the component that direct preference methods set out to remove. DPO uses a mathematical identity to fold the reward model into the policy loss, so preferences are learned in one supervised stage with no separate score predictor and no reinforcement learning loop, and the trade is real: a standalone reward model can score arbitrary new on-policy samples and supports online feedback collection, whereas DPO learns from a fixed comparison set. Reward models also serve beyond RLHF: a process reward model scores intermediate reasoning steps rather than only final answers, and reward models are used at inference time to rank candidates in best-of-N sampling. The preference labels themselves can come from humans or, in constitutional AI and RLAIF, from a model applying written principles.

image.alt: Human thumbs-up and thumbs-down preferences train a reward model, which then supplies the signal that trains a policy; labeled reward model and policy.
image.caption: Reward modeling learns a stand-in for human judgment: a model trained on people's comparisons of outputs, which then scores new outputs so a policy can be optimized against it. It is the heart of RLHF, and its central fragility too, since the policy will ruthlessly exploit any gap between the learned reward and what people actually wanted.
